questions.q: What is the primary architectural difference between monolithic and microkernel designs?
options: Monolithic kernels are faster but run on single-core systems; microkernel OSes support multi-core
Monolithic kernels include all services (file systems, drivers, scheduling) in one address space; microkernels move services to separate user-space servers
Microkernel means the kernel has fewer instructions but performs the same functions
They are equivalent designs with different performance characteristics
questions.explanation: Monolithic kernels (Linux, Windows) run all services in kernel mode in a single address space: file systems, device drivers, networking, scheduling. Microkernel designs (QNX, seL4) keep the kernel minimal, moving most services to privileged user-space servers. Monolithic kernels are faster (fewer mode switches) but a driver bug can crash the entire system. Microkernel OSes are more resilient: a crashed driver is restarted without affecting the kernel, but face message-passing overhead between kernel and servers.

questions.q: What is a system call and what happens during a system call transition?
options: A procedure call between two library functions
A controlled transition from user mode to kernel mode, allowing applications to request OS services with full hardware validation
An interrupt generated by a hardware timer
A context switch between two processes
questions.explanation: A system call is how user-mode processes request kernel services (read, write, fork, etc.). The process executes a SYSCALL instruction (or trap on older CPUs) with the system call number in a register. Hardware switches to kernel mode, jumps to a kernel dispatcher, which validates arguments (protection), performs the requested operation, and returns the result to user mode. This hardware-enforced transition is critical for security: the kernel validates all requests and can deny unsafe operations.

questions.q: What is the purpose of user mode and kernel mode privilege separation?
options: To assign different time slices to user and kernel code execution
To prevent unprivileged user code from executing privileged instructions or accessing protected memory, protecting the kernel and other processes
To allow users to debug their code more easily
To determine which processes get network access
questions.explanation: User mode restricts which instructions a process can execute (privileged instructions like I/O, interrupt masking, memory protection register modification are forbidden) and which memory it can access. Kernel mode allows all instructions and memory access. This hardware-enforced isolation prevents user code from corrupting the kernel, directly accessing hardware, or accessing other processes' memory. Transitions between modes occur via controlled entry points (system calls), not by user code.

questions.q: What is a loadable kernel module (LKM) and what are the security implications?
options: A user-space library that can be dynamically linked at runtime
Kernel code (drivers, file systems) that can be loaded and unloaded at runtime without rebooting, but with risks if untrusted code is loaded
A compiled kernel that is stored separately from the bootloader
A feature that allows users to modify kernel behavior
questions.explanation: Loadable kernel modules allow kernel functionality to be added without recompiling and rebooting. Linux, Windows, and many OSes support LKMs. Advantages: flexibility, faster development, reduced boot time. Security implications: LKM code runs in kernel mode with full privileges, so malicious or buggy modules can crash the system or compromise security. Many systems restrict LKM loading to administrators or use signed module verification. Microkernel OSes avoid this by running services in user space.

questions.q: What is the context of a process and what must be saved during a context switch?
options: The process's ranking in the ready queue
The CPU registers, program counter, memory management info, and other CPU state needed to resume execution
The list of files the process has open
The process's parent-child relationships
questions.explanation: A process's context includes: (1) CPU registers (general-purpose registers, stack pointer, frame pointer), (2) Program counter (PC), (3) Status register (flags, condition codes), (4) Memory management state (page table base), (5) Privilege level and mode. During context switch (from one process to another), the current context is saved to the outgoing process's PCB (process control block), and the incoming process's saved context is restored to CPU registers. This is expensive (cache pollution, TLB misses), motivating optimizations like process affinity.

questions.q: What is the kernel stack and how does it differ from the user stack?
options: They are the same; kernel stack is just another name for the user stack
The kernel stack is a separate, protected stack used by the kernel during system calls and interrupts; the user stack belongs to the user process
The kernel stack is temporary storage for boot; the user stack is used at runtime
The kernel stack stores instructions; the user stack stores data
questions.explanation: Each process has both a user stack (in user-mode virtual memory) and a kernel stack (in kernel-protected memory). When a system call or interrupt occurs, execution switches to kernel mode and uses the kernel stack for function calls and local variables. The kernel stack is protected from user code to prevent stack-based attacks. When returning to user mode, the kernel stack is unchanged for the next kernel entry. This separation prevents user code from corrupting kernel data structures.

questions.q: What is the purpose of the trap handler in the kernel?
options: To prevent processes from making system calls
To catch and handle software interrupts and system calls, validating arguments and dispatching to the appropriate kernel service
To monitor process execution for debugging
To manage memory allocation requests
questions.explanation: The trap handler (or syscall dispatcher) is kernel code that receives control when a process executes a TRAP (system call) instruction. It validates the system call number and arguments, then dispatches to the appropriate handler function (e.g., sys_read, sys_write). The trap handler is a critical security boundary: it validates that pointers are in user space, that file descriptors are valid, that sizes are reasonable, etc. A buggy trap handler can lead to security vulnerabilities.

questions.q: What are some performance issues with switching between user mode and kernel mode?
options: User mode is always slower than kernel mode regardless of operations performed
Mode switches involve CPU pipeline flushes, TLB (Translation Lookaside Buffer) misses, cache pollution, and interrupt disable/enable overhead
Mode switches only occur at boot time and have negligible cost
Performance issues only arise in multi-core systems
questions.explanation: Mode switches are expensive: (1) CPU pipeline flush—speculative execution must be discarded, (2) TLB flush—page table changes for kernel space, (3) Cache pollution—kernel code evicts user-code cache lines, (4) Interrupt masking—disabling interrupts to protect critical sections, (5) Register save/restore. Frequent system calls degrade performance. Optimization techniques include: batching system calls, providing user-mode libraries (VDSO on Linux), minimizing kernel work, and using faster synchronization.

questions.q: What is a process control block (PCB) and what information does it contain?
options: A circuit board used by the CPU to manage processes
A kernel data structure storing process state: registers, memory info, scheduling info, file descriptors, signal handlers, etc.
A command used to control process execution from the shell
A memory buffer for inter-process communication
questions.explanation: The PCB (or task struct in Linux) is a kernel data structure that represents a process. It contains: (1) Process ID (PID), (2) State (running, ready, blocked), (3) CPU context (registers, PC), (4) Memory management info (page tables, memory limits), (5) File descriptor table (open files), (6) Scheduling info (priority, time slices used), (7) Signal handlers, (8) Parent/child pointers. The OS maintains a PCB for each process, using it during context switches and resource management. PCBs are kept in kernel memory.

questions.q: What is a dual-mode kernel and what advantage does it provide?
options: A kernel that runs on two-core processors simultaneously
A kernel that supports both 32-bit and 64-bit processes
A kernel architecture with separate privileged (kernel) and unprivileged (user) execution modes to isolate and protect kernel code
A kernel with two different schedulers for different process types
questions.explanation: A dual-mode kernel leverages hardware support for kernel and user modes. Kernel mode allows all operations; user mode restricts privileged instructions and memory access. Advantages: (1) Protection—user processes cannot corrupt the kernel, (2) Security—the kernel can enforce isolation and access control, (3) Stability—a user process crash does not affect the kernel or other processes. Most modern kernels rely on dual-mode operation. Without it, any user code could disable interrupts or rewrite the kernel.
